must_not_assert: that certutil downloaded kxwn.lock from the network (EV-001 confirms that certutil created the file, but no network acquisition source is shown)
that the connection to 192.168.0.4:443 is confirmed command-and-control rather than a callback consistent with C2

the_trap: rundll32.exe (a signed Windows binary at C:\Windows\System32\rundll32.exe, spawned from explorer.exe) invites under-triage as routine system activity. Its command line loads C:\Users\user-01\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\kxwn.lock,VoidFunc: a DLL masqueraded with a .lock extension in a user AppData path, created earlier by certutil.exe (EV-001) and invoked through the VoidFunc export. The same rundll32 process (process_guid {8320f18b-2bcf-5ead-d200-000000000500}) then makes an outbound TCP connection to 192.168.0.4:443 (EV-002), consistent with a callback but not by itself proof of C2. EV-001 does not show how certutil obtained the file. Masquerade + LOLBIN DLL execution + outbound connection support the strong classification.
rationale: A0 shows rundll32.exe loading kxwn.lock through the non-standard VoidFunc export from C:\Users\user-01\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\. EV-001 confirms that certutil.exe created this DLL-like file with a .lock extension, but it does not establish that certutil downloaded it from the network. The same rundll32 process then makes an outbound TCP connection to 192.168.0.4:443 (EV-002), a private RFC1918 destination with no resolved hostname, consistent with an outbound callback rather than confirmed C2. The verdict rests on the masqueraded DLL execution, corroborated by the network connection: malicious, high; up-rank despite the signed rundll32 surface.
